reason: Deferred to v3 -- portability and ecosystem features are valuable but not foundational. Priority shifted to Tech Book Mastery (Phase 10, S131-S145).

stories.title: Obsidian vault export: notes, flashcards, and entity wikilinks as markdown files
stories.description: CONTEXT: Power users use Obsidian as their primary note-taking and knowledge management system. Exporting Luminary notes and flashcards as Obsidian-compatible markdown files enables the two tools to complement each other: Luminary for structured learning (FSRS, teach-back, gap detection), Obsidian for long-term knowledge management. USER JOURNEY: User opens Settings -> Export section -> clicks 'Export to Obsidian Vault' for a document -> downloads a .zip file -> unzips into their Obsidian vault folder -> notes appear as .md files with YAML frontmatter, flashcards appear in a flashcards.md compatible with the Obsidian Spaced Repetition plugin, entity wikilinks in graph.md enable Obsidian graph view. IMPLEMENTATION: New endpoint GET /export/obsidian?document_id=X. Generates a zip in memory: one .md file per note (YAML frontmatter: tags, document_title, created_at), flashcards.md with #flashcard tag format, graph.md with [[EntityName]] wikilinks.
acceptanceCriteria: GET /export/obsidian?document_id=X returns a .zip file with correct Content-Type
Zip contains one .md file per note with YAML frontmatter (tags, document_title, created_at)
Zip contains graph.md with [[EntityName]] wikilinks for each entity in the document's Kuzu graph
Frontend Settings page has 'Export to Obsidian' button per document in library
Button triggers zip download
uv run ruff check . exits 0; npx tsc --noEmit exits 0

stories.title: Anki deck export via genanki: flashcards as .apkg with FSRS state preservation
stories.description: CONTEXT: Anki is the global standard for spaced repetition with the largest mobile app install base. Exporting Luminary flashcards as an Anki .apkg deck solves the mobile FSRS gap (no mobile app needed) and lets users continue reviews on any device. FSRS difficulty and stability are stored as custom fields in the Anki note type so a future import back into Luminary can restore scheduling state. USER JOURNEY: User opens Learning tab -> clicks 'Export to Anki' in the flashcard panel -> downloads a .apkg file -> imports into Anki desktop or AnkiDroid -> all cards present with front (question) / back (answer + source citation). IMPLEMENTATION: uv add genanki. New endpoint GET /export/anki?document_id=X generates a .apkg. Custom Anki note model with fields: Front, Back, Source, FSRS_Difficulty, FSRS_Stability. Cards grouped into a deck named after the document title.
acceptanceCriteria: uv add genanki; listed in pyproject.toml
GET /export/anki?document_id=X returns a .apkg file
Deck name matches the document title
Each card has Front (question), Back (answer + source chunk citation), FSRS_Difficulty, FSRS_Stability fields
Anki package is valid: importable into Anki 2.1+ without errors
Frontend Learning tab flashcard panel has 'Export to Anki' button
Button triggers .apkg download
uv run ruff check . exits 0

stories.title: Local network relay for mobile FSRS review: LAN binding, QR code, and mobile study view
stories.description: CONTEXT: FSRS tethered to a desktop is the single biggest gap in the learning science model. Daily review adherence is what makes spaced repetition effective -- but the current app is only accessible from the computer where it runs. This story enables mobile browser access over a local WiFi network without any cloud infrastructure. USER JOURNEY: User opens Settings -> sees a QR code labeled 'Review on mobile' -> scans with phone on same WiFi -> phone browser opens a minimal mobile-optimized study page -> user reviews due cards with flip animation and FSRS rating buttons -> ratings sync instantly to the desktop database. IMPLEMENTATION: Backend: add --host 0.0.0.0 mode triggered by a LAN_MODE=true env var or Settings toggle. A new minimal frontend route /mobile/study renders a stripped-down FSRS review UI (no sidebar, no library, dark-mode safe). Settings page generates a QR code using a pure-JS library for the LAN URL.
acceptanceCriteria: Backend supports binding to 0.0.0.0 when LAN_MODE env var is set or Settings toggle is on
GET /study/mobile-session returns due cards formatted for the mobile view
Frontend /mobile/study route: card flip animation, FSRS rating buttons (Again/Hard/Good/Easy), no sidebar
FSRS ratings from mobile view persist in the same database as desktop sessions
Settings page shows a QR code with the LAN URL (http://192.168.x.x:8000/mobile/study)
CORS updated to allow LAN IP origin
Mobile view loads in < 3s on a mid-range phone over WiFi (no heavy bundle deps)
npx tsc --noEmit exits 0; uv run ruff check . exits 0

stories.title: Tauri v2 desktop packaging: single installer bundling frontend and Python sidecar
stories.description: CONTEXT: Currently the app requires the user to run uvicorn and npm run dev manually from a terminal. This is a significant barrier to adoption for non-developer users. Tauri v2 bundles the Vite frontend into a native desktop shell; the Python backend is packaged as a standalone binary (PyInstaller sidecar) spawned by Tauri on launch. The result is a single distributable installer for macOS (.dmg), Windows (.exe), and Linux (.deb). USER JOURNEY: User downloads Luminary.dmg, drags to Applications, opens it -- no Python or Node required. IMPLEMENTATION: uv add pyinstaller as a dev dependency. PyInstaller spec file that packages the uvicorn app as a standalone binary. Tauri v2 config: sidecar entry pointing to the binary, spawned on app launch and killed on app exit. GitHub Actions workflow builds and attaches installers on version tag push.
acceptanceCriteria: PyInstaller spec produces a standalone binary that runs the uvicorn app with no Python installation required
Tauri v2 tauri.conf.json configured with sidecar pointing to the Python binary
App launches from the installer: backend starts, frontend opens -- no terminal required
Sidecar process is killed cleanly when the Tauri window closes
GitHub Actions workflow: on tag push, builds .dmg (macOS), .exe (Windows), .deb (Linux) and attaches as release artifacts
Install on a clean macOS machine with no Python/Node: Luminary opens and basic ingestion works

stories.title: Readwise CSV and Kindle Clippings import with auto-ingestion pipeline
stories.description: CONTEXT: Many learners have years of book highlights stored in Readwise or Kindle. Importing them creates immediate value on first use -- the library is populated with their existing knowledge corpus before they upload a single new document. S123 adds basic Kindle Clippings parsing; this story adds the Readwise CSV format and ensures that imported highlights flow through the full ingestion pipeline (embedding, NER, FSRS card generation) so they are immediately searchable and learnable. USER JOURNEY: User opens Library -> Import -> 'Import from Readwise' -> selects a Readwise CSV export -> each book becomes a document with highlights as chunks, tags from Readwise preserved, author and book title populated -> user can immediately generate flashcards from their highlight library. IMPLEMENTATION: Readwise CSV columns: Title, Author, URL, Highlight, Note, Location, Date, Tags. Parse into document + chunk records. Run embed + NER pipeline on highlights. If document_title matches an existing library document, link highlights to it.
acceptanceCriteria: Import dialog in Library accepts Readwise CSV format
Parser extracts Title, Author, Highlight, Note, Tags from Readwise CSV columns
Each unique book title becomes one document; each highlight becomes one chunk
Imported documents flow through the full embed + NER ingestion pipeline
Tags from Readwise CSV are mapped to the Luminary document tags field
If document title matches an existing library document, imported highlights are linked to it
Flashcard generation works on Readwise-imported documents
Import progress shown in the library upload progress indicator
uv run ruff check . exits 0
